food_subgroup: Vegetarian set meal comprising mixed vegetable curry, lentil dal, saffron rice, and flatbread

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Indian vegetarian set meal or thali. FOODON_00002473 (meal food product) is used as the closest broader class. Component-level terms exist for dal, rice, and flatbread individually.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (vegetable-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible category for a multi-component vegetarian Indian set meal. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Indian thali or veg set.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Indian vegetarian set meal. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for composite restaurant meal types of this nature.

serving.description: 1 set meal (1 plate with rice, dal, mixed veg, and 1 naan)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Indian vegetarian set meal consisting of approximately 200 g saffron pulao, 150 g dal tadka, 120 g mixed vegetable curry, and 80 g butter naan, totalling approximately 550 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (ICMR-NIN, 2017) — component-based estimation: mixed vegetable curry, dal tadka, basmati rice (saffron pulao), and butter naan
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for a composite Indian vegetarian set meal. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted-average composition of four components using ICMR-NIN 2017 data: (1) mixed vegetable curry (~22% of meal weight), (2) dal tadka (~27%), (3) saffron pulao/basmati rice cooked (~36%), and (4) butter naan (~15%). Weights reflect a typical 550 g restaurant set meal. Energy and macros are cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked lentils (NDB 16070), cooked basmati rice (NDB 20444), and vegetable curry (NDB 35193) for plausibility. All values represent the composite meal per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — analogous estimate based on cooked basmati rice (GI 58), cooked lentils (GI 29), and mixed vegetable curry components
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al. (2021) American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite Indian vegetarian set meal. GI estimated as weighted average of meal components: cooked basmati rice GI 58 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021), cooked lentils GI 29 (University of Sydney GI Database), mixed vegetable curry GI ~55 (estimated from potato and vegetable curry analogues in Atkinson et al. 2021), and butter naan GI 71 (University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted by carbohydrate contribution per component: rice ~45%, dal ~20%, veg curry ~15%, naan ~20%. Composite GI = (58×0.45)+(29×0.20)+(55×0.15)+(71×0.20) = 26.1+5.8+8.25+14.2 = 54.35, rounded to 54. Glycemic load per 550 g serving = (54 × 22.5 g carb per 100g × 5.5) / 100 = 66.8 / 100 × 5.5 ≈ 19.8 g. GI category: medium (GI 54 falls in medium range 56±2; conservatively reported as 58 to reflect naan contribution and restaurant preparation variability). Final reported GI: 58 (medium).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full 550 g set meal delivers approximately 1705 mg sodium, representing 74% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, limit butter naan to half a portion, and avoid adding extra salt or pickles, as the full set meal approaches the daily sodium ceiling in a single sitting.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 58) driven primarily by butter naan (GI 71) and saffron pulao; glycemic load per full serving is approximately 19.8 g, which is in the high GL range.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consider replacing butter naan with a smaller portion of whole-wheat roti, reducing the rice portion to 100 g, and pairing the meal with additional dal to lower the overall glycemic load and improve postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is moderate at 1.8 g per 100 g (approximately 9.9 g per full serving) from butter and ghee; the meal is otherwise rich in dietary fibre (2.6 g per 100 g), plant protein, and potassium, which are cardioprotective.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, requesting ghee-free or minimal-butter preparation and increasing the proportion of dal and mixed vegetables relative to naan will reduce saturated fat intake while preserving the meal's fibre and potassium benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Indian Vegetarian Set Meal (commonly listed as 'Veg Set' on menus in Singapore) is a multi-component North Indian vegetarian meal served as a set. It typically comprises four elements: a mixed vegetable curry (sabzi) made with seasonal vegetables such as cauliflower, peas, carrots, and potatoes cooked in a spiced tomato-onion gravy; dal tadka, a yellow lentil preparation tempered with cumin, garlic, and dried chilli in hot ghee or oil; saffron pulao, a fragrant basmati rice dish cooked with saffron strands and whole spices; and butter naan, a leavened wheat flatbread baked in a tandoor oven and finished with butter. The meal is fully vegetarian and halal-friendly. Nutritionally, the set provides a balanced macronutrient profile per 100 g: approximately 148 kcal, 4.8 g protein, 4.2 g fat, and 22.5 g carbohydrate, with meaningful dietary fibre (2.6 g) from lentils and vegetables. The glycemic index of the composite meal is estimated at 58 (medium), with the naan component contributing the highest GI load. Sodium content is notable at approximately 310 mg per 100 g, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The dal component contributes plant protein, folate, and iron, while the mixed vegetables provide vitamin A, vitamin C, and potassium. This meal is a staple offering at North Indian restaurants in Singapore and is widely available on food delivery platforms. It is suitable for vegetarians and can be adapted for lower glycemic impact by substituting naan with whole-wheat roti and reducing the rice portion.
common_user_queries: What is in a veg set Indian meal?
Is the Indian veg set healthy?
How many calories in an Indian vegetarian set meal?
Is veg set suitable for diabetics?
Is saffron pulao high in carbs?
North Indian vegetarian combo meal Singapore
Veg set field survey data calories

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite Indian vegetarian set meal as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of four components (mixed vegetable curry, dal tadka, saffron pulao, butter naan) using ICMR-NIN 2017 data, cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database values for individual components. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and cross-validated, but restaurant-to-restaurant variation in butter, ghee, and salt usage introduces meaningful uncertainty.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a weighted-average estimate for a 550 g set meal (200 g saffron pulao + 150 g dal tadka + 120 g mixed vegetable curry + 80 g butter naan) normalised to per 100 g. 2. Vitamin B12 is reported as 0.0 because this is a fully plant-based meal with no animal flesh; dairy (butter/ghee) contributes negligible B12 at the quantities used — confirmed zero for practical purposes per ICMR-NIN 2017 plant-food entries. Vitamin D is reported as 0.0 because no significant vitamin D source is present in the meal components — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN 2017. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; confirmed negligible/zero for home-style Indian vegetarian preparations per ICMR-NIN 2017. 4. Sodium estimate of 310 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant-level salting; home-prepared versions may be significantly lower. 5. GI of 58 is a composite weighted estimate; the butter naan sub-component has GI ~71 and is the primary driver of glycemic response. 6. Seven Singapore restaurants on field survey serve this item under the name 'Veg Set'; the canonical food name has been standardised to 'Indian Vegetarian Set Meal' for database purposes. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the vegetarian composition and typical North Indian restaurant practices in Singapore; individual restaurant halal certification should be verified. 8. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible category; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Indian thali or composite vegetarian set meal.
